title: Project Home
text:
About The Project

This project explores disparities in breast and cervical cancer outcomes, with a focus on how access to healthcare influences screening and early detection. Using national data (Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System), I examine how factors like insurance, routine checkups, and having a personal doctor impact whether women receive life-saving screenings. 


Why This Matters
Breast and cervical cancers are among the most common cancers affecting women worldwide. While both are highly treatable, and in some cases preventable, when detected early, thousands of women still die each year due to delayed diagnosis and limited access to care. Additonally, many women are not aware of when or how to get screened. Gaps in health literacy and access to clear, culturally relevant information contribute to delayed diagnosis and missed prevention opportunities. These knowledge gaps, combined with structural barriers, continue to drive preventable deaths.


Who This Affects
These disparities disproportionately impact:

Women in low-income communities
Uninsured or underinsured individuals
Racial/ethnic minority populations
Women with limited access to routine healthcare/ education



Key Statistics

2.3 million new breast cancer cases globally each year 🔗
~670,000 breast cancer deaths annually 🔗
~660,000 cervical cancer cases worldwide 🔗
~350,000 cervical cancer deaths annually 🔗
Nearly 4,000 U.S. women die from cervical cancer each year 🔗

Despite cervical cancer being considered one of the most preventable cancers

title: About The Cancers
text:
Breast Cancer
Breast cancer occurs when abnormal cells grow uncontrollably in breast tissue. It is the most commonly diagnosed cancer among women in the United States and the second leading cause of cancer-related death. Early detection through routine mammograms significantly improves survival rates.
Some key risk factors include:

Increasing age
Family history and genetic mutations (such as BRCA1 and BRCA2)
Obesity
Alcohol use
Physical inactivity
Diet

While about 5–10% of breast cancers are hereditary, most cases occur in individuals without a known genetic mutation.
Like cervical cancer, breast cancer progresses through stages:

Stage 0–1: Cancer is localized within the breast
Stage 2–3: Tumors grow and may spread to nearby lymph nodes
Stage 4: Cancer spreads (metastasizes) to distant organs such as the bones, liver, lungs, or brain


Early detection is critical. Routine mammograms can identify breast cancer before symptoms appear, when it is most treatable and survival rates are highest.
Why this matters:
Although breast cancer is common, outcomes are significantly better when it is detected early. Delays in screening and diagnosis can lead to more advanced disease and poorer survival.


Cervical Cancer
Cervical cancer develops from abnormal cells in the cervix, often caused by persistent infection with high-risk types of human papillomavirus (HPV). It progresses slowly, which makes regular screening critical for early detection. 
Cervical cancer is one of the most preventable cancers, yet it continues to cause preventable deaths due to gaps in screening and vaccination.
Cervical cancer progresses through stages:

Stage 1: Cancer is limited to the cervix
Stage 2: Cancer spreads beyond the cervix but not to the pelvic wall
Stage 3: Cancer extends to the pelvic wall or lower vagina
Stage 4: Cancer spreads to other parts of the body


Because this progression is gradual, routine screening (Pap tests and HPV testing) can detect abnormal cells before they become cancerous. This makes cervical cancer one of the most preventable cancers.
Most cases of cervical cancer are caused by persistent infection with high-risk types of human papillomavirus (HPV), a common sexually transmitted infection. With HPV vaccination and regular screening, the risk of developing cervical cancer can be significantly reduced.
Why this matters:
When detected early, cervical cancer is highly treatable. However, lack of screening and awareness can lead to late-stage diagnosis, which is more difficult to treat and has worse outcomes.




Key Takeaway
Both breast and cervical cancers:

Have clear screening methods
Are highly treatable when detected early
Yet still cause preventable deaths due to gaps in access, awareness, and timely care

title: Data and Key Findings
text:
Among age-eligible women in BRFSS 2024, what healthcare access, demographic and socioeconomic factors are associated with being up to date on breast and cervical cancer screening?
Sub questions:

Does having a recent routine checkup make women more likely to be up to date on screenings?
Does having a personal doctor independently increase screening adherence?
Does having a personal care provider reduce screening gaps between insured and uninsured women?
Do cost-related barriers reduce likelihood of screening?
Do racial and ethnic disparities in screening persist after adjusting for healthcare access factors such as insurance status, having a personal doctor, and recent routine checkups?





This analysis used 2024 Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) data to examine cancer screening behaviors among age-eligible women in the United States.

Breast cancer (ages 50–74): ~71% up to date on mammography
Cervical cancer (ages 21–65): ~76% up to date on screening

title: Data and Key Findings
text:
Among age-eligible women in BRFSS 2024, what healthcare access, demographic and socioeconomic factors are associated with being up to date on breast and cervical cancer screening?
Sub questions:

Does having a recent routine checkup make women more likely to be up to date on screenings?
Does having a personal doctor independently increase screening adherence?
Does having a personal care provider reduce screening gaps between insured and uninsured women?
Do cost-related barriers reduce likelihood of screening?
Do racial and ethnic disparities in screening persist after adjusting for healthcare access factors such as insurance status, having a personal doctor, and recent routine checkups?





This analysis used 2024 Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) data to examine cancer screening behaviors among age-eligible women in the United States.

Breast cancer (ages 50–74): ~71% up to date on mammography
Cervical cancer (ages 21–65): ~76% up to date on screening

title: Data and Key Findings
text:
Key Findings

1. Routine Checkups Are the Strongest Predictor
Having a recent routine medical visit was the most influential factor associated with screening adherence.

Mammography: Over 4x higher odds of being up to date (OR ≈ 4.34)
Cervical screening: About 2x higher odds (OR ≈ 2.19) 

Takeaway: Routine healthcare visits are a critical for preventive care.



2. Having a Personal Doctor Improves Screening
Independent of checkups, having a consistent healthcare provider increases screening rates.

Mammography: OR ≈ 2.37
Cervical screening: OR ≈ 2.12 

Takeaway: Continuity of care matters as ongoing provider relationships support prevention and screening



3. Insurance Amplifies the Benefit of Provider Access
Having both insurance and a personal doctor produces stronger effects than either alone.

Uninsured + provider: modest increase (OR ~1.5)
Insured + provider: much stronger increase (OR ~2.0–2.3) 

Takeaway: Access works best when coverage + provider accessare both present.



4. Cost Barriers Reduce Screening—Even With Insurance
Financial barriers significantly decrease screening adherence.

Mammography: OR ≈ 0.60
Cervical screening: OR ≈ 0.85 

Takeaway: Insurance alone is not enough because out of pocket cost may still limit and defluence obtaining care



5. Disparities Persist Across Racial/Ethnic Groups
After adjusting for access and socioeconomic factors, disparities still remain.

Breast cancer screening:

Higher odds among Black women (OR ≈ 1.33)
Lower odds among several other groups (Asian, American Indian/ Alaska Native, multiracial)

Cervical cancer screening:

Most racial/ethnic groups had lower odds compared to White women
Largest gaps observed among Asian and AIAN populations 


Takeaway:
While access improves outcomes, it does not fully eliminate disparities, suggesting additional structural, cultural, and informational barriers.

title: Data and Key Findings
text:
Big Picture Takeaways

Access to care (checkups, insurance, providers) strongly drives screening behavior
Financial barriers remain a major obstacle even among insured individuals
Disparities are multifactorial, extending beyond access alone
Improving outcomes requires addressing:

Healthcare access
Cost barriers
Health literacy and awareness gaps

title: About Me
text:
Hi, I’m Bianca McAlister, an MPH student at Morehouse School of Medicine. I am passionate about women’s health, cancer prevention, and advancing health equity in underserved communities. My work focuses on understanding how access to care shapes health outcomes and how we can reduce preventable disease through early detection and education.



MPH Candidate, Epidemiology Affinity Group
Morehouse School of Medicine
Expected Graduation, May 2026



Research Interests: Women’s Health, Chronic Disease, Cancer Prevention
Public Health Issues of Concern: Access to care, Reducing chronic illness, Drug Epidemic in the United States
Population of Interest: Women, Underserved Populations, Unhoused Populations

title: Prevention and Screening
text:
Prevention
HPV Vaccination

Protects against high-risk HPV types responsible for most cervical cancers
Recommended to begin vaccination at ages 11–12
HPV causes over 90% of cervical cancers

There are over 200 strains of HPV, yet the following are the most high risk

12 high-risk HPV types: HPV 16, 18, 31, 33, 35, 39, 45, 51, 52, 56, 58, and 59

HPV Strains 16 and 18 are responsible for the most HPV related cancers





Lifestyle Factors (Breast Cancer)

Maintain a healthy weight
Limit alcohol intake
Stay physically active
Breastfeeding (when possible) may reduce risk



Screening
Mammography (Breast Cancer)

Recommended starting around age 40 (varies by guideline)
Detects cancer early before symptoms appear
Early-stage detection = significantly higher survival


Cervical Cancer Screening

Pap tests and HPV testing
Recommended for women ages 21–65
Detects abnormal cells before they become cancer

title: Disparities and Why They Exist
text:
Even with effective prevention tools, not everyone benefits equally. Breast and cervical cancers are among the most preventable and treatable cancers, yet not everyone has the same opportunity to benefit from early detection and care.
Disparities in access to care lead to some women being more likely to be diagnosed at later stages, experiencing delays in treatment, or having worse outcomes. These differences are not random, as they reflect broader inequities in access to healthcare, information, and resources.

title: Data and Key Findings

Big Picture Takeaways

Access to care (checkups, insurance, providers) strongly drives screening behavior
Financial barriers remain a major obstacle even among insured individuals
Disparities are multifactorial, extending beyond access alone
Improving outcomes requires addressing:

Healthcare access
Cost barriers
Health literacy and awareness gaps

title: Resources and Recommendations
text:
Based on this research, improving breast and cervical cancer outcomes requires more than medical advances it requires improving access, awareness, and trust in the healthcare system.



Increase access to routine care

Expand availability of primary care providers
Encourage annual checkups as a gateway to prevention

Reduce financial barriers

Expand low-cost and free screening programs
Improve insurance coverage and affordability

Strengthen continuity of care

Increase access to personal healthcare providers
Support long-term patient-provider relationships

Improve health literacy and awareness

Provide clear, culturally relevant education on screening
Increase awareness of HPV, Pap tests, and mammograms
Ensure patients understand when and why to get screened

Address structural inequities

Invest in underserved communities
Expand mobile clinics and community-based care









1. Grady Health System (Atlanta)

Offers breast and cervical cancer screenings
Sliding scale fees and financial assistance available
Strong safety-net system serving uninsured populations
👉 https://www.gradyhealth.org


2. Fulton County Board of Health

Breast and cervical cancer screening programs
Family planning + preventive services
Multiple clinic locations across Atlanta
👉 https://fultoncountyboh.com


3. DeKalb County Board of Health

Pap tests, HPV services, and women’s health screenings
Low-cost services for eligible residents
👉 https://dekalbhealth.net


4. Georgia Breast and Cervical Cancer Program (BCCP)

FREE breast & cervical cancer screenings for eligible women
Covers mammograms, Pap tests, and diagnostic follow-up
Can also help with treatment coverage if diagnosed
👉 https://dph.georgia.gov/bccp





5. Black Women’s Wellness (Atlanta-based)

Focuses on health equity for Black women
Provides education, navigation, and screening support
👉 https://bwwellness.org


6. Sisters by Choice

Breast cancer support, education, and advocacy
Focus on early detection in Black communities
👉 https://sistersbychoice.org


7. HEAL Clinic (Morehouse School of Medicine)

Student-run clinic providing free care to underserved populations
Screenings, education, and referrals





8. American Cancer Society (Atlanta HQ)

Screening guidelines
Patient navigation support
Cancer education resources
👉 https://www.cancer.org


9. CDC Cancer Prevention Resources

HPV vaccination info
Screening recommendations
👉 https://www.cdc.gov/cancer







Mammogram: Starting around age 40 (talk to your provider)
Pap test: Ages 21–65
HPV vaccine: Ages 11–12 (can be given later if missed)





Early detection saves lives….but only if people have:

Access to care
Affordable services
Clear information
Support to navigate the system

Prevention is possible. Equity is the missing piece.

title: Disparities and Why They Exist
text:
What the Data Shows
Findings from this study highlight that access to healthcare plays a major role in whether women receive recommended cancer screenings:

Women with a recent routine checkup had significantly higher screening rates
Having a personal healthcare provider increased the likelihood of screening
Insurance coverage improved screening adherence
Cost-related barriers reduced the likelihood of being up to date on screening

At the same time, disparities across racial and ethnic groups persisted, even after accounting for some access-related factors, indicating that inequities are multi-factorial and deeply rooted.

title: Disparities and Why They Exist
text:
Key Drivers of Disparities

1. Access to Healthcare
Access to care is one of the strongest predictors of screening.
Individuals without:

Health insurance
A regular healthcare provider
Routine medical visits

are significantly less likely to receive preventive screenings.
Routine checkups often serve as the primary entry point into the healthcare system, where screenings are recommended, scheduled, and completed.



2. Financial Barriers
Cost remains a major obstacle, even for those with insurance.
Out-of-pocket costs, copays, transportation, and time off work can prevent individuals from seeking care. It many times becomes a domino effect.



3. Health Literacy and Knowledge Gaps
Access alone is not enough—individuals must also understand when and why to seek care.
Many people:

Are unaware of recommended screening guidelines
Do not fully understand the purpose of Pap tests or mammograms
Lack knowledge about HPV and its link to cervical cancer

Without clear, accessible, and culturally relevant health information, individuals may delay or forgo screening—even when services are available.



4. Continuity of Care and Trust
Having a consistent relationship with a healthcare provider increases the likelihood of screening.
Continuity of care:

Builds trust
Encourages preventive care conversations
Improves follow-up after abnormal results

However, many individuals—especially those in underserved communities—lack access to a regular provider.



5. Structural and Social Determinants of Health
Disparities are also shaped by broader social and environmental conditions, including:

Income and socioeconomic status
Education level
Geographic location (urban vs. rural access)
Transportation barriers
Historical and systemic inequities in healthcare

These factors influence both access to care and health outcomes.

title: Disparities and Why They Exist
text:
Why This Matters
When people face barriers to care, screening is delayed and cancers are more likely to be diagnosed at later, more serious stages.
For example; Black women experience significantly higher rates of delayed diagnosis and treatment for breast and cervical cancer compared to white women, contributing to a 38% higher breast cancer death rate and higher mortality for cervical cancer. 🔗

title: Disparities and Why They Exist
text:
Connecting It All Back
Your findings reinforce a key public health takeaway:

Improving access to care significantly increases screening rates
But access alone does not fully eliminate disparities

This means that effective solutions must address:

Healthcare access
Financial barriers
Health literacy and education
Structural inequities

title: Disparities and Why They Exist
text:
Barriers to Care

Lack of health insurance
Cost-related barriers
No regular healthcare provider
Limited access to routine checkups
Health literacy gaps
Structural and systemic inequities


What the Data Shows

Women with recent checkups are significantly more likely to be screened
Having a personal doctor increases screening rates
Cost barriers reduce the likelihood of screening
Racial disparities decrease when access to care is equalized

title: Disparities and Why They Exist
text:
Why Do These Disparities Exist?

Access to Healthcare
Not everyone has consistent access to care.
People without:

Health insurance
A regular healthcare provider
Routine medical visits

are less likely to receive recommended screenings like mammograms and Pap tests.
For many, a routine checkup is the first step in accessing preventive care and without it, screening opportunities are often missed.



Cost and Financial Barriers
Even when services exist, they may not be affordable.
Barriers can include:

Out-of-pocket costs
Lack of insurance coverage / Full coverage insurance
Transportation expenses
Time off work

These challenges can delay or prevent individuals from seeking care altogether.



Health Literacy and Awareness
Understanding healthcare is just as important as accessing it.
Many individuals:

Do not know they need to be screened
Are unsure when to start screening
Do not know how often screenings are needed
May not understand the purpose of tests like Pap smears or mammograms
Are unaware of HPV and its role in cervical cancer
May have been misinformed or have stigma around the HPV vaccination

Without clear, accessible information, people may not seek care…even when it is available.



Continuity of Care and Trust
Having a consistent relationship with a healthcare provider matters.
A trusted provider can:

Recommend screenings
Answer questions
Provide follow-up care
Understand medical history and social determinants

However, many individuals especially in underserved communities lack access to ongoing, consistent care.



Structural and Social Factors
Health outcomes are shaped by more than individual behavior.
Broader factors include:

Income and socioeconomic status
Education
Neighborhood and geographic location
Access to transportation
Historical and systemic inequities in healthcare

These conditions influence both access to care and health outcomes over time.

title: Data and Key Findings
text:
Big Picture

Access to care (checkups, insurance, providers) strongly drives screening behavior
Financial barriers remain a major obstacle even among insured individuals
Disparities are multifactorial, extending beyond access alone
Improving outcomes requires addressing:

Healthcare access
Cost barriers
Health literacy and awareness gaps
